title: Supraworld

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

overall_note: The rail has very strong top-end fits led by the obvious Supraland comps, plus a solid broader first-person metroidvania neighbor. It gets looser in the back half where puzzle-first or exploration-action games overlap only partially with Supraworld’s specific ability-gated puzzle-metroidvania identity.
anchor_summary: Supraworld is a first-person puzzle-metroidvania built around dense interconnected exploration, ability-gated traversal, secret hunting, low-emphasis combat, and very light guidance. Best matches should preserve that first-person exploratory puzzle loop and progression structure more than just sharing a thoughtful or exploratory vibe.
gap_notes: The strongest expected comps are the existing Supraland games, and those are present.
There is a noticeable drop after the top three from first-person puzzle-metroidvania into broader first-person exploration/action or pure puzzle games.
Some candidates share first-person discovery and light guidance, but not the same progression structure or combat/exploration balance.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-identical gameplay identity: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile ability unlocks, dense secrets, toy-world traversal, minimal hand-holding, and light combat.
Same series formula and almost the same audience expectation: gated first-person exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, constant optional secrets, and even less combat emphasis.
A strong first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability-gated backtracking, though it is more combat-driven and less puzzle-centric than Supraworld.
Shares first-person exploration, traversal-tool unlocks, revisiting areas, and secret hunting, but its structure leans more action-adventure than puzzle-metroidvania and supports co-op.
Good first-person environmental puzzle overlap and low hand-holding, but it lacks the metroidvania backbone, traversal gating emphasis, and exploratory secret-hunting loop that define Supraworld.

title: Marathon

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

overall_note: The proposed rail gets the core extraction-shooter space right at the top with two very strong comps, but the Warzone pick is weakened because the game’s broader identity is battle royale/combat arena and the extraction similarity is mode-specific rather than the primary gameplay identity.
anchor_summary: Marathon is a first-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around infil/scavenge/exfil runs, gear risk, squad-or-solo play, hostile AI plus rival players, build/loadout customization, and persistent seasonal progression between matches.
gap_notes: Top two candidates align strongly with Marathon’s extraction loop, PvPvE pressure, and risk-reward progression.
Hunt matches session tension and squad-vs-environment-vs-rival structure especially well, despite a very different pacing and weapon model.
Escape from Tarkov is an essential benchmark for the extraction format, though it is much more hardcore/tactical/sim-oriented than Marathon appears to be.
Call of Duty: Warzone 2.0 is only a partial fit because the extraction comparison depends on DMZ-style structure rather than the title’s main enduring identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest genre benchmark: first-person extraction raids, PvPvE threats, gear loss on death, prep/loadout strategy, and progression driven by successful exfils. More hardcore and sim-leaning than Marathon, but still an essential comp.

Very close on match structure and tension: squad-or-solo PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival teams, and extraction under pressure. Slower weapons and horror-western flavor differ, but the core loop fits strongly.

There is some extraction overlap via its objective/sandbox mode history, but Warzone’s core identity is battle royale/combat arena, not persistent risk-based extraction progression. Understandable reference, but loose for a primary similar-games rail.

title: ARC Raiders

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's extraction-shooter identity well at the top, especially with PvPvE raid-and-extract games that feature between-run stash or contract progression. The main weakness is that several candidates are first-person and/or much harsher tactical sims than ARC Raiders, while The Division 2 is more of a persistent looter-shooter neighbor than an extraction comp.
anchor_summary: ARC Raiders is a third-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around repeated raids into hostile maps, scavenging loot, surviving machines and rival players, then returning to a hub/workshop for crafting, repairs, trader quests, skill progression, and gear improvement; it supports solo or up-to-3-player play and leans on tactical gunplay plus gadgets.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are games with explicit PvPvE extraction loops and between-run progression; those are appropriately represented here.
Perspective matters for this anchor: third-person candidates get extra credit, while first-person extraction games are still valid but slightly looser fits.
ARC Raiders' mix of hostile AI creatures/machines plus rival players is better matched by The Cycle: Frontier than by PvP-heavier extraction games like Vigor.
The Division 2 shares third-person shooting and progression structure, but its core loop is not extraction-driven, so it is notably weaker than the others.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on core loop: sci-fi PvPvE drops for loot, hostile creatures plus players, extraction, contracts, and between-run gear progression. First-person perspective keeps it just short of a same-tier match.

Strong third-person extraction fit with scavenging runs, bring-home loot, and shelter upgrading. Slightly looser because it is more PvP-centric and less defined by co-op PvPvE enemy ecology than ARC Raiders.

A foundational extraction-shooter comp with raids, stash, traders, quests, and high-stakes loot loss. It is a bit less aligned due to first-person hardcore mil-sim emphasis and a more punishing sim/RPG layer than ARC Raiders.

Clear PvPvE extraction structure with AI threats, stealth/noise management, and squad extraction tension. Less close because its boss-hunt objective structure, old-weapons gunplay, and harsher permadeath flavor differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader loot-progression loop.

Reasonable third-person shooter neighbor thanks to cover combat, co-op, loot, and hub-style progression, but its core identity is a persistent looter-shooter/open-world RPG rather than a raid-extract-loss loop.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a classic Call of Duty package centered on first-person gunplay: a co-op/solo campaign with covert military missions, competitive multiplayer with 6v6 and 20v20 maps, and zombies as an additional mode. The copy emphasizes near-future weaponry, mastering an arsenal, Omnimovement, multiplayer map counts, and a signature campaign/multiplayer structure, which strongly supports military_fps over horror-led archetypes. Zombies and psychological-war themes are present, but they read as one component of a broader shooter package rather than the core genre identity of the whole title.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity. It correctly prioritizes other Black Ops entries that share the campaign/multiplayer/Zombies bundle, with Modern Warfare III as the closest non-Black Ops franchise neighbor.
anchor_summary: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 is best understood as a full-package military FPS: first-person gunplay across a cinematic/co-op campaign, competitive multiplayer, and round-based Zombies, with Black Ops-style near-future movement and progression.
gap_notes: Black Ops 6 is the clearest essential comparison and should anchor the rail.
The strongest fits here are driven by the shared three-pillar Call of Duty structure rather than just franchise branding.
Modern Warfare III fits as a looser Call of Duty package comp, but it is less exact because its co-op/Zombies structure differs from Black Ops 7's round-based emphasis.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Immediate predecessor with the same campaign/multiplayer/round-based Zombies package and very similar Black Ops gunplay, pacing, and player expectations.

title: Making*Lovers: First Blush

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored Steam description explicitly says 'Read a Romantic Comedy' and 'Standard Visual Novel Mechanics,' describing text reading, skipping previously read text, and hiding the text window to view artwork. The content is centered on an authored romance story with four heroines, no combat, and single-player progression through narrative scenes, which strongly matches the visual_novel archetype.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity as a romance-focused Japanese-style visual novel. The base game is an essential inclusion, and the rest are all credible neighboring multi-route romance VNs with similar reading-first structure and heroine-route progression.
anchor_summary: Making*Lovers: First Blush is a single-player romance visual novel centered on reading a lighthearted, multi-heroine romantic comedy with standard VN presentation, route-based progression, and no action-oriented systems.
gap_notes: Making*Lovers should be treated as the obvious must-include benchmark because First Blush appears to be a direct companion/prequel/spinoff within the same gameplay lane.
The remaining candidates are all good fits, but they are broader genre neighbors rather than as exact a match as the base Making*Lovers title.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct Making*Lovers counterpart and the clearest comparison point: same romance-VN format, heroine routes, comedic tone, and dating-focused progression.
Very close fit as a contemporary romantic-comedy visual novel with heroine selection, interactive route progression, and a pure-love focus.
Another light multi-heroine romance VN with the same reading-driven structure and character-route emphasis; slightly less direct than Fureraba but still highly aligned.
Still a solid romance VN comp with route-based heroine stories and authored progression, though its elite-academy setup makes it a touch less everyday-dating aligned than the others.

title: Powerwash Simulator 2: Adventure Time Pack DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is insufficient for taxonomy classification. The title suggests DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, but no usable gameplay description, mechanics summary, perspective, session structure, or parent-game evidence is provided in the stored DB. The only stored genre/theme signal is a broad 'simulation' label from OpenCritic, which is too generic to map confidently to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype.

overall_note: The rail is tightly focused on the PowerWash Simulator family, which is directionally correct. The main gap is that only one candidate is a true parent/base-game match for this DLC; the rest are mostly neighboring franchise entries or DLCs for the prior game rather than equally direct pairings for PowerWash Simulator 2 content.
anchor_summary: Anchor is a licensed DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, so the best comps are the PowerWash Simulator 2 base game first, then other PowerWash entries and themed DLCs that preserve the same first-person pressure-washing, grime-clearing job loop and relaxed progression structure.
gap_notes: PowerWash Simulator 2 is the essential inclusion and should clearly lead the rail as the base-game match.
The PowerWash Simulator 1 DLC packs are very close on core loop, but they are still add-ons for the previous base game rather than direct sibling DLCs for PowerWash Simulator 2.
The original PowerWash Simulator is highly relevant, but it is a series predecessor rather than as direct a match as the anchor's actual parent game.

title: Solid Void - Topsy Turvy World

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored evidence clearly describes a standalone single-player puzzle game built around dual-layer nonogram and jigsaw challenges across 250+ puzzles, with calming presentation and difficulty ramping. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a general nonogram/jigsaw puzzle category, and the game does not materially fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, or any other listed archetype. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: The set has several strong nonogram and art-jigsaw neighbors, but it misses an exact hybrid comp that combines both halves of the anchor’s core loop. Best fits are pure nonogram titles first, then calm image-assembly puzzlers, with narrative-heavy picross hybrids ranking below them.
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player puzzle game built around a dual-step loop: solve nonogram grids, then use the revealed information to complete jigsaw-style image assembly across 250+ puzzles with a gentle difficulty ramp and artwork restoration framing.
gap_notes: No candidate matches the anchor's defining two-step structure of nonogram followed by jigsaw assembly in the same puzzle flow.

title: Minos

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's reverse-dungeon/trap-defense identity reasonably well, especially with games about constructing killboxes and stopping intruders. The main gap is that several candidates drift toward broader dungeon management RTS or direct action tower defense rather than Minos's specific maze-building roguelite loop.
anchor_summary: Minos is a single-player maze-building roguelite focused on engineering labyrinth layouts, placing and chaining traps, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. Its identity leans more toward systemic trap construction and reverse-dungeon defense than direct action combat.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-identical benchmark for Minos's specific combination of maze construction, trap chaining, and roguelite runs.
The set leans partly toward adjacent dungeon-defense and trap-action games, with less emphasis on editable labyrinth design.
Some candidates over-index on direct combat or macro-management compared with Minos's engineering-first structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on building lethal maze-like spaces with modular traps and routing intruders, though its PvP raiding/FPS play and asynchronous multiplayer focus make it less roguelite and less purely defensive than Minos.
Strong reverse-dungeon-defender match with roguelite structure, trap prep, and stopping hero incursions, but it is more management-heavy and turn-based than Minos's maze-engineering sandbox.

Shares evil-dungeon construction, trap placement, and defending against heroes, but its RTS/god-game economy, minion management, and broader strategy scope pull it away from Minos's run-based maze-building identity.

A sensible trap-defense comp because of chained hazards and wave stopping, but it is primarily direct action/tower defense with fixed lanes and active combat rather than player-authored labyrinth building.

title: Town of Zoz

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's food-plus-action-town loop reasonably well, but it leans too heavily on adjacent food/business hybrids and misses a truly near-identical benchmark. The best candidates are the action RPG/life-sim hybrids with strong town relationship structure; the more roguelite, puzzle, or shopkeeping-first games are looser fits.

title: Missing The Point

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short, low-stress single-player fishing-focused experience with handcrafted art, river drifting, landmark observation, and decisions that influence the outcome. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a fitting fishing-sim or contemplative narrative-sim bucket, and the evidence does not strongly support mapping it to an existing archetype such as life_sim or visual_novel without overreaching. The title appears to center on relaxed fishing and light branching narrative rather than the defining gameplay identities represented in the taxonomy.

overall_note: The rail has a solid fishing-centered core and mostly stays within cozy or narrative-adjacent fishing games, but several candidates drift toward heavier RPG, management, or survival structures than the anchor’s notably short, low-pressure, contemplative identity.

title: Prop Sumo

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Prop Sumo as a 'party fighting game' centered on PvP matches where players brawl with melee moves, chain light and heavy attacks, use items, and compete for score across arena maps. The Steam detailed description adds strong fighting-game signals: 1v1 and free-for-all match formats, a Training Dojo to master moves and combos, unlockable ultimates, and round-based progression. This supports a primary identity of traditional_fighter, with party_game as a secondary flavor due to chaotic multiplayer arenas, items, and friend-focused free-for-all play.

overall_note: The rail has several strong party-brawler comps and one genre benchmark. Best fits emphasize arena knockouts, items, hazards, free-for-all structure, and casual-competitive multiplayer rather than traditional 1v1 fighter depth.
anchor_summary: Prop Sumo is a multiplayer party brawler: short PvP arena matches with melee combos, charge attacks, items, hazards, score-chasing, customizable rules, and light progression/unlocks. Its identity sits between party game chaos and an arena/platform-fighter-like competitive loop.
gap_notes: The set leans heavily toward physics-party brawlers, which fits the social chaos side of Prop Sumo well.
There is only one clear arena-fighter benchmark; more games with structured versus combat, items, and rules customization would better cover Prop Sumo's combo/dojo side.
Candidates focused on objective or heist loops fit less well than pure score-based elimination brawlers.

title: Beyond Words

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Beyond Words as a word-based strategic puzzle roguelite: 'fusion of roguelike strategy and word-crafting,' with runs shaped by letter placement, modifiers, stickers, booster/power cards, upgrades, synergies, seeded runs, time attacks, and boss challenges. The gameplay loop is tactical board optimization and build construction across repeated runs, with no combat signals. Strong fit for word_puzzle_strategy rather than action, simulation, or hidden-object categories.

overall_note: The set finds the anchor’s wordplay-plus-buildcraft identity reasonably well, with the best fits coming from other word-driven upgrade/loop games. title: Incantation

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Incantation as a first-person horror mystery game centered on exploring an eerie cult village, searching for clues, hiding from danger, and uncovering the truth behind a missing daughter. The repeated emphasis on fear, madness, mystery-solving, and stealth-based survival supports psychological_horror as the best fit, with stealth_action_adventure as a secondary influence. Evidence for combat-driven survival-horror is weak, so the classification stays with horror exploration and stealth rather than action combat.

overall_note: The set is directionally solid for first-person narrative horror, especially around vulnerability, investigation, and supernatural pursuit. title: Pokemon Champions

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a multiplayer-focused Pokémon battle product centered on traditional Pokémon battle mechanics, with Ranked, Casual, and Private Battles plus Pokémon HOME compatibility. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a strong fit for a match-based turn-based monster battling game without the broader campaign, exploration, or creature-collection loop expected by monster_collect_rpg. Accepting it under an existing archetype would overstate unsupported elements such as adventure structure or collection gameplay.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core because it includes battle-centric Pokémon references and modern competitive mainline entries, but it mixes in campaign-heavy monster-collection RPGs that are less aligned with Champions’ battle-platform identity.
anchor_summary: Pokémon Champions is a multiplayer-first, match-based Pokémon battler built around traditional turn-based Pokémon combat, team building, and online ranked/casual/private PvP rather than campaign exploration or creature-catching progression.

Temtem is a reasonable adjacent comparison for turn-based creature-team PvP, but it is structurally much farther away because it is an MMO-style campaign creature-collection RPG.

title: The Occultist

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. The gameplay loop centers on exploring Godstone, investigating the protagonist's father's disappearance and occult rituals, using a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and crucially avoiding direct combat: 'Alan always avoids direct combat' and progresses via 'Hide and Sneak' stealth mechanics. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

overall_note: The set contains several credible first-person horror neighbors, with Outlast and Maid of Sker fitting especially well. The rail weakens after that because Visage is closer in atmosphere than in game structure, and Call of Cthulhu leans more toward narrative investigation/adventure-RPG than stealth-forward survival horror.
anchor_summary: The Occultist is a first-person survival-horror game centered on tense exploration, occult investigation, environmental interaction via a special tool, and stealthy avoidance of supernatural threats instead of direct combat. Best matches should share first-person horror structure, vulnerable/low-combat play, narrative progression through exploration, and survival tension more than just occult theme.
gap_notes: There is no must-keep benchmark here despite the anchor's clear first-person stealth-survival-horror identity.
Top candidates appropriately emphasize vulnerability and enemy avoidance rather than combat.
Lower-ranked entries drift toward psychological exploration horror or investigative adventure rather than the anchor's explicit hide-and-sneak survival loop.

title: Samson

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

overall_note: The rail has two strong gameplay comps for Samson’s blend of open-world action, melee, and vehicular combat, but one candidate is weakened substantially because it is a DLC entry rather than the actual base game comparison a player would expect.
anchor_summary: Samson appears to be a single-player open-world urban action-adventure built around third-person brawler combat, consequential mission/job selection under time/debt pressure, police escalation, city traversal, and weaponized driving as a major secondary verb rather than a pure racing loop.
gap_notes: A must-include style comparison is present in Sleeping Dogs, which is the clearest urban open-world brawler/driving comp here.
Mad Max is a strong structural neighbor because it also combines on-foot melee progression with vehicle combat, though its wasteland survival structure is less street-crime focused than Samson.
Mafia III: Stones Unturned is the wrong product-level match; the base game Mafia III would be more appropriate than a specific DLC expansion.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent fit: open-world city structure, story-driven crime missions, strong hand-to-hand combat emphasis, driving integrated into progression, and police heat all align closely with Samson’s stated loop.

Very strong neighbor for the mix of brutal melee, upgrade progression, open-world roaming, and vehicles used directly as combat tools, though its desert survival framing is less urban and less mission-consequence driven.

The underlying Mafia III base game has some overlap, but this specific candidate is a DLC episode, not the main game, and its heavy-weapons focus is a weaker match for Samson’s core brawler-and-driving identity.

title: Starfield - Terran Armada

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

overall_note: The rail has the essential base game and several sensible western/sci-fi RPG neighbors. The main weakness is that Skyrim is represented by the VR edition rather than the standard release, which introduces a mode/profile mismatch.
anchor_summary: Starfield - Terran Armada is a story DLC for Starfield, so the closest comparison is the parent game's Bethesda-style single-player open-world sci-fi RPG structure: first/third-person questing, character building, dialogue-driven roleplay, exploration, shooting, loot/crafting, and long-form progression.
gap_notes: Starfield is an essential base-game inclusion and should remain the clear #1.
Fallout 4 and The Outer Worlds are the best non-parent comps because they closely match the first-person single-player quest-driven RPG loop.
Mass Effect: Andromeda is a valid but looser neighbor due to its more directed structure and party-based action-RPG identity.
The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim VR is only a partial fit because the underlying Bethesda RPG formula is relevant, but the VR-specific mode/profile makes it less clean than standard Skyrim would be.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game of this story DLC; same systems, structure, progression, and audience expectation.

Spacefaring action RPG with exploration, missions, combat, and progression, but it is more directed and squad/story focused than Bethesda's sandbox RPG loop.

title: ChainStaff

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid for brutal 2D action-platformers, especially candidates emphasizing combat-heavy campaigns and boss fights. Best fits match the anchor’s authored side-scrolling action loop more than broader exploration-heavy or loot-driven variants.

title: Eko and the Bewitched Lands

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct for a fast action-roguelite rail. The best fits are other top-down, run-based combat games with dodge timing, build variation, and meta progression. The main gap is that none of the candidates fully capture Eko's lighter action-adventure/puzzle angle, and several candidates lean more into co-op or hero-based structures than the anchor appears to.
anchor_summary: Top-down or overhead-feeling single-player action roguelite/action RPG: fast dodge-heavy real-time combat, randomized runs/worlds, per-run build upgrades, weapons/abilities/artifacts, bosses, and permanent meta progression, with some light puzzle/exploration flavor.
gap_notes: No candidate clearly mirrors the anchor's action-adventure plus puzzle/exploration component; most comps are more combat-room-forward.
Several candidates emphasize co-op or multiplayer as a notable mode profile, while the anchor is presented as single-player.
The strongest matches are roguelite action games rather than a pure loot_action_rpg reading, suggesting the anchor's closest practical neighborhood is action roguelite/top-down hack-and-slash.

title: Machine Child

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Machine Child as a non-combat girl-raising/nurturing simulation. The gameplay loop centers on guiding a daughter through life in a town via 700+ story events, choosing activities, outfits, and developmental paths, building affection, and reaching 22 different endings. That strongly fits life_sim with branching, relationship-driven progression and replayable single-player outcomes. Visual-novel adjacency is present through authored event scenes and multiple endings, but the core identity is life simulation rather than pure VN.

overall_note: The rail is well targeted. It includes the core daughter-raising benchmarks first, then broader stat/choice-driven raising sims. The biggest strength is that the top candidates share Machine Child's central identity of shaping a girl's future through repeated developmental choices and branching endings.
anchor_summary: Machine Child is a single-player daughter-raising life sim: guide a girl through authored events, choose activities and outfits, build affection, shape stats/pathing, and reach many branching endings. Best matches should share the parenting/raising loop, schedule or choice-driven development, and replayable outcome-focused progression rather than just general narrative or fantasy vibes.
gap_notes: Princess Maker-style games are the clearest benchmark space for this anchor, and the proposed set correctly centers that lineage.
Volcano Princess and Ciel Fledge fit especially well because they preserve the explicit daughter-raising structure while modernizing presentation and systems.
Long Live The Queen is a valid looser comp due to weekly planning, stat checks, and branching outcomes, but it is more survival/queen-training than nurturing daughter-raising.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Foundational daughter-raising sim with the closest shared loop: schedule/stat management, bonding, events, and many adulthood outcomes shaped by your guidance.

title: Sigma Star Saga DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. While the evidence is strong for the game's mixed identity, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not cleanly cover this exploration-plus-shmup structure. Candidate RPG fits in the computed taxonomy are incomplete and rely on weak or mismatched assumptions about combat structure and perspective. Because its core gameplay loop materially spans an unsupported genre combination rather than aligning strongly to a single allowed archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The rail has one very strong genre-hybrid comp, one decent structural neighbor, and one broader action-RPG pick that is understandable but noticeably looser. It captures the anchor's mixed action/shooter identity better than a pure RPG rail would, but only The Knight Witch really speaks directly to the anchor's exploration-plus-shmup blend.
anchor_summary: Sigma Star Saga DX is a rare hybrid built around two equally important modes: top-down action-RPG/adventure exploration with tool-gated progression, quests, bosses, and branching story, plus frequent side-scrolling shmup combat with ship customization. Best matches should reflect that dual-mode campaign identity rather than just sci-fi setting or generic action-RPG overlap.

title: KuloNiku: Bowl Up!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a restaurant-management/cooking simulation loop: inherit and restore a family restaurant, take customer orders, prepare meals through tactile cooking actions, upgrade tools and ingredients, customize the shop, and earn gameplay benefits from decorations. Progression is driven by improving the restaurant and building friendship levels with town residents, with a light authored story around rivals and family legacy. Although 'Meatball Brawls' are framed competitively, they are still culinary scoring duels rather than combat, so the core identity remains management sim/tycoon rather than action. Evidence is strong enough to accept management_tycoon, with some life-sim adjacency from relationship-building and town social elements.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's restaurant-management core well, especially order fulfillment, shop upgrading, and hands-on cooking. Best matches are other single-player food-service sims with progression and customization. The main weakness is one candidate that shifts heavily toward co-op roguelite/efficiency play rather than cozy career-style restaurant progression, and there is no truly obvious must-keep benchmark beyond the strongest restaurant-sim neighbors provided.

title: People of Note

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify People of Note as a turn-based RPG centered on recruiting a band of party members, traveling across multiple themed regions, and progressing through a story campaign. The combat loop is explicitly party-based and tactical, with timing-based rhythm inputs layered onto turn-based encounters via musical mashup attacks and changing combat conditions. This supports a JRPG-story-RPG identity first, with tactical RPG elements secondary. The rhythm component appears additive to the core party-managed campaign RPG structure rather than defining the game as a standalone rhythm genre.

overall_note: The rail has several solid JRPG comps, especially games with turn-based party combat and active timing inputs. It is weaker on exact musical-rhythm JRPG parallels, and one candidate leans too much on theme over combat structure.

title: Hidden around the World

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure built around exploring hand-drawn city scenes, finding hidden objects, completing relaxing missions, unlocking new scenes, and decorating locations. The gameplay loop is discovery/puzzle-oriented rather than combat- or platform-focused, with single-player progression across handcrafted destinations. Metacritic also explicitly labels the genre as Hidden Object, reinforcing hidden_object_puzzle as the best fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's hidden-object identity. The best matches emphasize calm scene-scanning, hand-drawn environments, progression through stages, and in several cases creation/sharing features that strongly mirror the anchor's level-sharing hook. The only looser fit is the more point-and-click adventure-leaning Please, Touch The Artwork 2.
anchor_summary: Cozy single-player hidden-object puzzle/adventure centered on scanning hand-drawn scenes, completing relaxed find missions, unlocking new locations, adding light decoration/customization, and supporting user-created level sharing.
gap_notes: A direct must-keep comparison is present in Hidden in My Paradise, from the same creators and with nearly identical find/decorate/share structure.
The rail skews toward pure hidden-scene search games; that is appropriate for the anchor's core loop.
Only one candidate is meaningfully adjacent rather than tightly matched: Please, Touch The Artwork 2 adds more adventure/puzzle tasking beyond straightforward hidden-object scanning.

title: A Rat's Quest: The Way Back Home - Season 1

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic copy consistently describe a single-player, narrative-driven action adventure centered on movement and navigation: 'climb, jump, crawl, slither, and sneak,' with explicit 'hybrid gameplay' switching between side and top-down perspectives. Combat is clearly present but not dominant over traversal, with a sword, throwing daggers, survival against threats, and 'challenging boss encounters.' The game is framed as a cinematic authored journey with voiced cutscenes, supporting a long-form linear campaign. While the cinematic framing is strong, the core playable identity in the evidence is platform-navigation-led action with stealth elements, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's small-creature scale, authored adventure structure, and traversal-plus-stealth emphasis reasonably well, but several picks drift toward puzzle/VR, heavy combat RPG, or low-challenge exploration rather than the anchor's action-platforming core.
anchor_summary: Single-player cinematic action-platformer/action-adventure with a tiny rodent protagonist, hybrid side/top-down traversal, climbing/crawling/sneaking through oversized spaces, light-to-moderate combat with boss fights, and a linear story-driven campaign.
gap_notes: There is no near-identical hybrid 2D/overhead cinematic action-platformer in the set, so the rail leans on broader tiny-hero action-adventures.
Some candidates match the miniature-animal perspective well but miss the anchor's combat/challenge profile or stealth-platforming structure.
The strongest fits are the games centered on vulnerable small protagonists navigating dangerous oversized spaces with stealth, traversal, and story focus.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Tiny rodent-led single-player action-adventure with strong stealth, agile navigation, dangerous oversized spaces, and a story quest; less platformer-structured and more immersive sim/RPG, but very close in audience and play feel.

Very close on cinematic traversal through hostile oversized environments, squeezing/climbing/hiding, and linear tension-driven progression; weaker on direct combat and lacks the rodent/adventure-romance identity.

Shares storybook tiny-hero action-adventure, traversal, combat, and environmental navigation, but VR framing and player-assist puzzle structure make its core loop meaningfully different from the anchor's standard action-platformer campaign.

Matches side-scrolling small-rat action, dangerous creatures, and boss-centric progression, but it is much more combat-heavy, RPG-geared, and punishing than the anchor's traversal-stealth-cinematic balance.

Good miniature-animal perspective and narrative exploration overlap, but it is a gentle quest/puzzle adventure with little combat, little stealth tension, and no tough action-platformer challenge model.

title: All Will Fall

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify All Will Fall as a 'survival city builder/colony sim' centered on physics-based 3D construction, resource management, logistics, production chains, societal needs, research progression, random events, and sandbox/scenario play. The core loop is building and optimizing a settlement rather than commanding armies or pursuing direct combat. Overhead strategy presentation, base/colony growth, systemic simulation, and optimization pressure strongly support city_builder as the primary archetype, with colony_sim and factory_builder as secondary overlaps due faction needs, survival management, and supply-chain emphasis.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong for survival city-builder fans, with good coverage of colony management, pressure, and scenario-driven settlement optimization. Its main gap is that only Timberborn closely reflects the anchor’s standout 3D/vertical construction and systemic building-physics identity; the rest lean more toward broader survival city-building than that specific structural sandbox niche.
anchor_summary: All Will Fall is a single-player survival city builder/colony sim focused on overhead settlement construction, resource logistics, faction/population needs, research, scenario/sandbox play, and a distinctive physics-based vertical building system where structural collapse is a core challenge.
gap_notes: The set underrepresents games with comparable vertical/stacked construction as a primary mechanic.
Only one candidate meaningfully echoes the anchor’s physics-driven building and collapse considerations.
Most candidates fit survival colony management well, but several are looser on sandbox construction freedom and logistics-led city design.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on survival city-building, vertical architecture, systemic resource chains, faction differences, and environmental simulation; especially strong because 3D construction and water/terrain systems mirror the anchor’s structural-planning appeal.

Excellent match for scenario-driven survival city management, population needs, harsh tradeoffs, and difficult policy decisions, though it is less of a freeform construction sandbox and lacks the anchor’s vertical physics-building focus.

Good fit through pressure-heavy city/colony management, infrastructure balancing, exploration, and survival decision-making, but its closed habitat structure and space-station management are less construction-sandbox-driven than the anchor.

Solid post-apocalyptic colony builder with research, expeditions, disasters, and population management, but it is more conventional settlement survival and less defined by vertical building, physics, or dense logistics construction.

title: SHINOBI: Art of Vengeance - SEGA Villains DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive rather than mode-shifting: it adds 5 stages, 3 bosses, ninpos, skins, music, and boss rush modes. The parent game's stored description explicitly defines the core gameplay as 'an all-new 2D action platformer' with precision ninja combat, combos, obstacle-overcoming tools, challenging platforming puzzles, hidden secrets, and multiple stages. That strongly supports action_platformer with side-scrolling 2D stage-based progression, dominant combat, platform traversal, boss encounters, and precision platforming. The DLC's boss-rush/stage content reinforces rather than contradicts that identity.

overall_note: The set has the essential base-game pairing and several credible 2D ninja/action-platformer neighbors. The biggest weakness is structural drift: some candidates lean more toward metroidvania exploration or stealth than the anchor’s combat-forward stage-based action-platformer identity.
anchor_summary: A DLC expansion for a 2D single-player ninja action-platformer built around side-scrolling stages, precision combat, traversal challenges, boss fights, and additive progression content like new stages, bosses, ninpo, and boss rush.
gap_notes: The base game is correctly included and should be mandatory for a DLC anchor like this.

title: Cursed Words: The Word Game That Isn't

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cursed Words as a roguelike word/strategy game built around repeated runs, stage-based progression, synergy-building upgrades, and rule-bending modifiers. The Steam detailed description gives concrete loop evidence: each run has five stages with encounters and a boss, victory at Stage 5, challenge tiers afterward, and build-defining systems via Character, Sticker upgrades, Stamp loadout, and consumable Tiles. This strongly supports a strategic word-puzzle identity with roguelite/deck-growth structure rather than an action genre. Combat is metaphorical ('boss encounters') but the gameplay described is non-combat, methodical, and score/synergy focused.

overall_note: The rail has one excellent structural comp and one strong word-mechanics comp, but it is thin and misses more direct roguelike word-run peers. Balatro fits the anchor's run structure and score-synergy identity very well despite using cards instead of words; Letter Quest fits the word-combat layer well but is less aligned on roguelike run structure and build-crafting depth.
anchor_summary: A single-player roguelike word-puzzle strategy game built around repeated runs, stage-and-boss structure, and heavy synergy construction through persistent loadout choices and run-specific modifiers that bend the rules of word formation and scoring.
gap_notes: The set is small and lacks a near-direct roguelike word-game comparator.
There is only one candidate that clearly matches the anchor's run-based synergy-exploitation structure.
Word construction is represented, but long-form roguelite progression and challenge-tier structure are under-covered.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent match on run-based structure, escalating score thresholds, boss/blind checkpoints, and rule-breaking synergy builds. Different verb set than word play, but the gameplay identity is very close.
Strong overlap in forming words to drive encounters and buying upgrades between battles, but it reads more like a turn-based word RPG/endless mode game than a deep roguelike synergy-run builder.

title: ROGOLF

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the game as a minigolf roguelite: each run is different, levels have their own rules, players spend shots as score, collect coins for multipliers, buy equipment/power-ups, and progress through gates toward higher stages. This is strong evidence for a run-based score-chasing sports/arcade structure with roguelite modifiers. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a fitting minigolf/golf-arcade/roguelite sports archetype. 'sports_sim' is a poor fit because the game is not presented as simulation-focused, and other archetypes are even less appropriate. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: The rail has a solid gameplay center around arcade/minigolf, with one especially strong roguelite comp. However, most candidates miss ROGOLF's run-based upgrade/progression identity and lean more toward pure multiplayer minigolf, score attack, or comedy/puzzle reinterpretations of golf.
anchor_summary: ROGOLF is a run-based arcade minigolf roguelite: physics-driven hole clearing, shot/score efficiency, coin collection, equipment or power-up purchasing, changing level rules, and gated progression across runs. Best matches should preserve both the minigolf core loop and the roguelite/arcade progression structure, not just golf theme.
gap_notes: Only Cursed to Golf strongly matches both golf play and roguelite/run-based progression.
The set over-indexes on conventional or party minigolf, which captures shot physics and obstacle courses but not the shop/equipment/gated-run structure.
There is no must-keep base benchmark for the specific minigolf-roguelite niche beyond the top candidate.
What the Golf? shares constant rule remixing, but its comedy-puzzle identity is much looser than ROGOLF's score-driven minigolf runs.

Strong minigolf physics and obstacle-course identity make it understandable, but its multiplayer-party focus is a notable mismatch with ROGOLF's single-run progression and upgrade economy.

title: Breath of Fire IV

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Breath of Fire IV as a classic single-player JRPG adventure with a fixed cast of party members, skill and magic mastery, strategic battles, dragon transformations, and an authored story following Ryu, Nina, and companions across a fantasy world. Steam and Metacritic both support a long-form campaign structure and JRPG identity; the gameplay copy emphasizes party-based strategic combat and story progression, not match-based or fighting-game play. The current computed near-miss for traditional_fighter appears to come from misleading phrases like 'go head to head' and 'combo actions,' but the broader evidence clearly supports a story-driven party RPG.

overall_note: The rail is directionally correct and both candidates fit the anchor’s gameplay identity as story-driven late-PS1-style JRPGs. However, the set is small and leans broad rather than capturing Breath of Fire IV’s especially close fixed-party, turn-based, classic JRPG profile as tightly as possible.

gap_notes: Both candidates are valid JRPG comps, but neither reflects the specific dragon-transformation hook directly.
The proposed relationships underrate the closeness a bit; these are stronger than merely loose adjacent matches.

title: Resident Evil 3: Nemesis

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports classic survival horror. Steam and corpus text explicitly state third person perspective, horror/zombie themes, escaping Raccoon City alive, and relying on brute force and cunning against terrifying mutations. Metacritic directly says it is part of the series that made the survival horror genre. The descriptions imply a linear authored campaign with encounter-driven enemy pressure, deliberate survival-oriented combat, and a single-player focus; Mercenaries Mode adds a secondary match-style mode without changing the core identity.

title: Resident Evil 2 (1998)

The presentation is described as cutting-edge 3-D/photorealistic for its era, supporting third-person 3D survival-horror identity. title: Resident Evil (1996)

title: Morkull Ascend to the Gods

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 2D side-scrolling action title centered on platforming and repeated boss battles. The strongest signals are side-scrolling presentation, dominant melee combat, boss-centric structure, and a single-player campaign loop. The copy emphasizes combos, unlockable skills and charms, and preparing builds before battles, which supports action-platformer progression with some metroidvania/beat-em-up adjacency. However, exploration, backtracking, and discovery are not described strongly enough to make metroidvania the primary archetype, and the boss-focused structure is less typical of a pure beat 'em up.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's main lane of 2D action-platforming with boss emphasis and melee combat fairly well. Best fits are the direct predecessor and nearby boss-heavy melee platformers; weaker fits drift toward broader exploration-first metroidvanias or boss-centric games with a notably different combat model.
anchor_summary: Morkull Ascend to the Gods looks like a 2D side-scrolling action-platformer built around melee combat, repeated boss attempts, light build customization via skills/charms, and some platforming/exploration, with a strong boss-centric rather than exploration-centric structure.
gap_notes: A direct predecessor is present and should clearly lead the rail.

title: TOMAK: Save the Earth Regeneration

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as visual_novel based on the strongest stored evidence: Metacritic explicitly labels the game as 'Visual Novel', and the only available description centers on a non-combat, narrative-driven cohabitation/relationship premise over a fixed three-year story arc. The text supports authored story progression, interpersonal focus, and a single long-form campaign. The computed JRPG/monster-collect signals appear over-inferred from sparse text and are contradicted by the direct genre label plus the absence of concrete evidence for combat, party control, or creature collection.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid because all three candidates emphasize character management, time progression, and branching narrative outcomes over combat. The best matches are raising/life sims with structured schedules and multi-year growth arcs; the looser fit is a modern partner-management sim that shares the care/choice loop but differs more in tone and activity structure.

title: Fishbowl

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call Fishbowl a 'visual novel' and a narrative slice-of-life coming-of-age story. The core loop centers on making choices over the course of a month, video-calling friends/family/coworkers, doing daily care/work tasks, and sorting through belongings and memory-related puzzles. The text stresses 'no bad endings, just different paths' and says 'the choices you make will shape the possibilities in Alo's future,' which strongly supports authored branching and dialogue/relationship-focused interaction. No combat is described anywhere; instead the gameplay is framed as intimate, cozy, grief-focused narrative progression with light puzzle/matching activities. That combination fits visual_novel much better than broader adventure labels.

overall_note: The proposed rail has a solid grasp of Fishbowl's intimate narrative, cozy slice-of-life tone, and memory/grief focus. Its best candidates match the anchor's low-pressure, story-first structure, but several picks drift toward broader emotional-adventure or domestic-puzzle neighbors rather than close gameplay identity matches.

title: Clean Up Earth

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a relaxing environmental restoration/cleanup game focused on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged structures, exploring large maps, and cooperating with up to 25 players. The strongest supported traits are multiplayer/co-op support, large shared spaces, casual/simulation framing, and non-combat restorative play. However, the Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a suitable home for a cooperative cleanup/restoration exploration sim with minimal or no combat. The closest available archetypes are poor fits because they require identity-defining elements not supported here, such as farming/life loops, factory/engineering systems, colony/city management, survival pressures, shooter combat, or RPG progression. Because the gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the available archetype set, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core around cleanup-and-restoration games with visible world recovery, but it skews heavily toward single-player neighbors and lacks a truly close multiplayer/community-scale comparator. The top picks fit the anchor’s core loop well, while the lower picks drift toward broader action-adventure or farming hybrids.
anchor_summary: Clean Up Earth is a relaxing restoration/cleanup sim built around vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged areas, exploring maps, and watching immediate environmental recovery. Its identity is non-combat, tool-based cleanup with solo/co-op/community multiplayer support and large shared restoration spaces.
gap_notes: Most candidates match the cleanup/restoration fantasy but not the anchor’s notable co-op/community multiplayer profile.

title: Darwin's Paradox!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: most candidates capture the anchor's authored platform-adventure structure, puzzle-forward progression, and stealthy escape through dangerous environments. The main gap is perspective/gamefeel spread—some picks are 2D cinematic puzzle-platformers while others are broader 3D exploration adventures—so the set mixes very close structure comps with looser animal-protagonist thematic neighbors.
anchor_summary: Darwin's Paradox! reads as a single-player cinematic action-platformer/adventure built around traversal, trap avoidance, light stealth, and environmental puzzle-solving in an authored campaign. Its identity is less combat-driven than movement- and escape-driven, with bespoke creature abilities used to infiltrate and survive a hostile industrial space.

Available simulation-adjacent archetypes such as management_tycoon, life_sim, farming_sim, engineering_sandbox_sim, or transport_sim would misrepresent the game's primary hands-on culinary simulation loop.

anchor_summary: First-person, hands-on cooking/restaurant simulation centered on realistic food prep, kitchen station management, recipe execution/customization, customer service pressure, progression, and optional co-op/sandbox chaos.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call the game a 'Metroidvania adventure' with a 'vast, non-linear world' that is 'seamlessly connected.' They describe classic side-scrolling platformer play, ability-gated progression via toys and pajamas, exploration of interconnected domains, boss fights, collectibles, and unlocking new abilities to access new pathways. That combination strongly supports metroidvania over a simpler action-platformer label.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: most candidates are colorful, approachable 2D exploration platformers with ability unlocks and boss progression. Best fits are other light-to-midweight metroidvanias with strong traversal gating. title: Last Man Sitting

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a roguelite action shooter built around surviving progressively harder waves, leveling up during runs, drafting power-ups, unlocking chair upgrades, and finding build synergies. The core loop is presented as single-player or up to 4-player co-op, with PvP arena/king-of-the-hill described as an extra mode rather than the main identity. Combat is dominant, fast, and hybridized between shooting and hacking/slashing, with minibosses and bosses closing out wave progression. This strongly fits co_op_action_roguelite over arena_fps because the text emphasizes roguelite run structure, power-up drafting, co-op survival, and meta-upgrades rather than primarily match-based FPS competition.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around co-op roguelite shooters with build-focused runs and horde pressure. Its biggest gap is that only one candidate strongly reflects the anchor’s 4-player co-op, run-based horde survival from a third-person/arena-feeling angle; the rest skew more corridor/level-based FPS or single-player arcade action.
anchor_summary: Last Man Sitting is best read as a co-op action roguelite shooter: survive escalating enemy waves, level during runs, draft power-ups, build synergies, and use persistent upgrades/loadouts in solo or up-to-4-player co-op, with PvP as a side mode rather than the core identity.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches the anchor's specific mix of wave-survival arena structure, 4-player co-op, and third-person shooter/melee hybrid combat.
The set leans heavily toward FPS roguelites, which fit progression and run structure but miss some of the anchor's broader arena-survival identity.

title: Subliminal

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Subliminal as a first-person psychological horror experience centered on exploring handcrafted open-ended memory-spaces and solving light/perspective-based environmental puzzles. The Steam detailed description emphasizes hallucination-like instability, unsettling spaces, a pursuing inhuman figure, and psychologically terrifying encounters, which strongly supports psychological_horror over action_horror or survival_horror. Gameplay identity appears to be exploration plus puzzle-solving under threat rather than combat-driven play; chase/pressure sequences justify light survival-oriented encounter framing, but not dominant combat. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'First-Person Adventure,' while Steam copy repeatedly stresses psychological horror, environmental puzzle solving, and single-player progression through authored spaces.

overall_note: The rail has a solid core around first-person reality-warping puzzle play and psychological horror exploration, but it includes one clearly wrong title and misses a cleaner distinction between mechanical puzzle comps and horror-under-threat comps.
anchor_summary: Subliminal appears to be a single-player first-person psychological horror game built around exploration and environmental puzzle-solving, especially light and perspective manipulation, with intermittent chase/threat sequences rather than combat-heavy survival systems.
gap_notes: Superliminal and Viewfinder are the best mechanical matches because Subliminal's identity strongly emphasizes perspective/light-based spatial puzzles.

The Complex is a poor fit here because it is an interactive movie/choice-driven thriller rather than first-person exploratory puzzle horror.

title: Raccoin: Coin Pusher Roguelike

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives strong gameplay evidence: this is an arcade-style coin pusher built around repeated runs, score-chasing, and physics-driven coin drops, with roguelike deckbuilding layered on top through collectible special coins, 150 items, character-specific loadouts/playstyles, endless mode, and multiple difficulty levels. It is not literal pinball, but within the available taxonomy the closest fit is pinball because the core interaction is a non-combat arcade table/machine focused on chaining effects, manipulating a physical playfield, and maximizing payouts/scores. Secondary card_battler reflects the explicit 'roguelike deckbuilder' structure, though card play itself is absent, so it remains secondary rather than primary.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's unusual mix of arcade randomness/physics and roguelike buildcrafting reasonably well, but it leans a bit too heavily on broad 'dopamine roguelite' overlap. The strongest fits are other run-based games where a machine-like randomizer or physics board is the main interaction; more combat-centric or purely card-scoring comps are slightly looser.

The strongest gameplay identity is therefore soulslike_action_rpg rather than metroidvania: the game is explicitly top-down rather than side-scrolling, and the copy emphasizes boss-centric challenging combat, build/loadout variety, and soulslike structure more than platforming. Metroidvania remains a strong secondary influence because the store text explicitly cites 'Metroidvania elements' and an interconnected world with secret optional areas.

title: Homura: The Crimson Warriors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is sufficient to accept this as a visual novel. Metacritic explicitly labels the game's genre as 'Visual Novel,' which is strong direct evidence. The descriptive copy is entirely story- and character-focused, emphasizing Nobushige Sanada, the Ten Braves, their bonds, and a historical Sengoku-era narrative aimed at otome players. No concrete evidence supports action, strategy, simulation, or combat-driven play, and the current audit already flags combat_presence as none plus authored_linear and historical signals. Together this supports a single-player, non-combat, historically themed visual novel identity.

overall_note: The rail has several strong otome VN comps centered on historical Japan and route-based narrative play. The main weakness is one broader romance VN pick that matches format more than the anchor's specific historical-war drama focus, plus one candidate with noisy metadata that still appears to fit well from description text.

One candidate's stored taxonomy/text is noisy and conflicts with its actual description, but the descriptive evidence still supports inclusion as a VN neighbor.

title: Grime II

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify GRIME II as an 'action-adventure metroidvania' and describe hallmark genre structure: side-scrolling exploration through a deep interconnected world, secret paths gated by newly acquired forms/abilities, platforming traversal, and combat used both for progression and discovery. Combat is a major pillar, with parry/grasp mechanics, enemy absorption, summoned molds, environmental combat, special attacks, and epic bosses, which also supports a soulslike-leaning secondary identity. The world is presented as a handcrafted surreal space with distinct locations and discovery emphasis. Evidence is strong enough to accept metroidvania as the primary gameplay archetype.

overall_note: The rail is very strong overall: it includes the essential base game and several close 2D combat-centric metroidvania or soulslike-adjacent comps that match GRIME II’s exploration/combat identity well.

title: The Posthumous Investigation

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored descriptions provide solid high-level identity: a single-player hand-drawn noir time-loop murder mystery set in 1937 Rio, centered on learning character schedules, linking evidence on a Thinking Board, reconstructing a timeline, manipulating routines, and solving the case through logic. However, the evidence does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It strongly suggests investigative adventure / detective puzzle design, but there is no explicit support for the required loops of hidden_object_puzzle (hidden-object scenes or point-and-click object hunt), visual_novel, immersive_sim_action, or other available archetypes. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet sits outside the current archetype list, classification should be rejected rather than forced into a mismatched bucket.

title: Enter the chronosphere

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player, procedurally generated run-based top-down shooter/roguelike with a distinctive hybrid structure: turn-based tactical planning blended with real-time bullet-hell action, plus build synergies, unlockable characters, weapons, and missions. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a good-fit archetype for a single-player tactical/bullet-hell roguelike shooter of this kind. Available shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, tactical_shooter, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or extraction_shooter do not match the top-down run-based roguelike identity, and turn_based_tactics would also be misleading because the game is explicitly a shooter with hybrid real-time bullet-hell combat rather than primarily overhead squad tactics. Because the gameplay evidence is strong but does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's hybrid tactical-shooter identity reasonably well, with especially strong matches around time-linked action, run-based combat, and top-down sci-fi roguelike structure. The main gap is that only a couple candidates really reflect the anchor's exact blend of bullet-hell shooter feel and move-to-advance tactical pacing; several others match the roguelike/top-down shooter side but lose the anchor's signature temporal combat model.
anchor_summary: Enter the Chronosphere is a single-player top-down roguelike shooter built around procedurally generated runs, ranged combat, exotic gear synergies, unlockable characters/progression, and a distinctive hybrid timing model where action advances when you move, creating turn-based tactical bullet-dodging inside a shooter structure.
gap_notes: The rail has no other candidate that combines top-down shooting with the exact 'everything moves when you do' structure as directly as SUPERHOT does conceptually.
Several candidates fit the run-based/top-down shooter roguelike layer but not the anchor's hybrid turn-based bullet-hell pacing.
The set leans heavily on sci-fi/top-down action comps, but some are more about pause tactics, stealth, or fully real-time gunplay than the anchor's specific combat cadence.

title: Super Meat Boy 3D

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly define the game as a tough-as-nails 3D platformer centered on 'straight forward twitch reflex platforming' and 'precision platforming action.' The text emphasizes brutal but fair levels, repeated deaths from hazards, wall-leaping, and escalating difficulty, which strongly matches precision_platformer. Metacritic genre labeling supports third-person 3D platforming, while the copy's references to 'a bunch of levels,' Dark World levels, and boss fights support a level-based campaign structure with boss-centric encounters. Combat is not described as a primary loop, so the game fits precision platformer far better than action_platformer or 3d_collectathon; unlockable secrets are present, but collection is not framed as the core objective.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core around precision platforming, especially the original Super Meat Boy and Celeste. Its main weakness is perspective/structure drift: several candidates are 2D or mix in exploration/combat more heavily than the anchor’s straightforward 3D hazard-platforming identity.
anchor_summary: Super Meat Boy 3D is a single-player, level-based 3D precision platformer built around brutal-but-fair hazard gauntlets, rapid retries, mastery-heavy movement, and twitch-reflex execution, with boss fights and secrets as secondary elements rather than combat or collection as the core loop.
gap_notes: There is only one clear 3D precision-platforming comp here; the rest mostly compare on challenge model rather than perspective.
The strongest fit is the original Super Meat Boy, which feels like an essential benchmark and should lead the rail.
Blue Fire is a useful 3D comp, but its metroidvania exploration, combat, and RPG-like progression make it looser than the best candidates.
Crash Bandicoot 4 fits level-based 3D platforming better than some 2D precision comps, but it is less purely precision-driven and more mainstream action-platformer in feel.
N++ matches fast deaths and short challenge rooms well, but its minimalist physics-puzzle flavor and 2D presentation make it a more stretched neighbor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct predecessor and clearest benchmark: same hazard-dense, mastery-heavy, instant-retry precision platforming loop, just in 2D rather than 3D.
Extremely strong precision-platforming comp with exact movement, rapid respawns, handcrafted challenge rooms, and repeated-death mastery, though it remains 2D and more narrative-driven.
One of the better 3D movement-skill comps, especially for trap-heavy platforming gauntlets, but combat, exploration, quests, and progression are much more central than in the anchor.

Strong overlap in short punishing stages, lethal hazards, and skill-based retries, but its minimalist 2D physics-platformer identity is notably farther from the anchor’s 3D Meat Boy structure.

title: Legacy of Kain: Ascendance

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'fast, 2D action platformer' centered on vertical movement, fluid combat, and skill-driven play. Multiple sources reinforce side-scrolling/2D platforming, while the Steam detailed description adds concrete combat signals: chaining melee strikes, evasive dashes, supernatural attacks, and level-based environmental challenges/puzzles flowing into combat. The copy also supports a linear campaign-like single-player structure, dark fantasy setting, pixel art presentation, and high-execution platforming/action focus. Evidence is strong and internally consistent for action_platformer rather than metroidvania or a pure platformer.

overall_note: The set stays mostly within 2D combat-platformer territory and includes several credible comps for a stage-driven action-platformer. title: Hozy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Hozy as a cozy restoration/decorating simulation focused on cleaning, painting, furnishing, and interacting with objects across nine restoreable locations, with no timers, scores, penalties, or combat. The evidence is strong for a non-combat cozy renovation sim loop, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not provide a clear fit for this gameplay identity. It is not well-supported as life_sim, management_tycoon, hidden_object_puzzle, or any action/adventure/RPG/shooter/racing archetype. Because the gameplay is clear yet falls outside the available archetypes, this should be rejected rather than forced into a poor match.

overall_note: The set captures Hozy's renovation/cleanup identity well at the top, especially with House Flipper and its sequel. The rail gets weaker once it shifts from interior renovation/decorating toward pure cleaning or broader environmental restoration.
anchor_summary: Hozy is a cozy first-person restoration sim built around low-pressure cleaning, painting, repairing, and decorating interior spaces room by room, with tactile tools, creative furnishing, and no combat or fail-state pressure.
