title: Aether & Iron

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives clear gameplay identity: a single-player narrative RPG where you recruit companions, level abilities, make consequential choices, and engage in 'tactical turn-based combat' with an 'interesting twist on strategic, turn-based vehicular battles.' The text repeatedly emphasizes crew management, tactical encounters, mystery-solving in an alternate 1930s floating New York, and choice-driven story progression. That supports turn_based_tactics as the core play identity, with western_narrative_rpg as a secondary layer.

overall_note: The rail has several credible tactical-RPG neighbors, but it mixes in a couple of narrative-investigation comps that miss the anchor’s party-tactics core. Best fits are squad-based, story-led turn-based RPGs; pure narrative detectives are more adjacent than central.

None of the candidates directly mirror the anchor’s vehicular tactics angle, so the rail is leaning on broader party-based tactical RPG identity instead.

title: MARVEL MaXimum Collection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame MARVEL MaXimum Collection as a retro compilation/archive containing multiple distinct legacy Marvel games and versions across arcade, 8-bit, 16-bit, and portable platforms. The text explicitly says it is 'spanning multiple genres' and then lists materially different included games and modes: beat-'em-up action (X-Men: The Arcade Game), a platforming variant of Captain America and The Avengers, co-op side-scrolling action in Maximum Carnage/Separation Anxiety, and an infamously difficult shooter in Silver Surfer. Because the package aggregates several different gameplay identities rather than presenting one dominant, unified core loop, there is not a single taxonomy archetype that can be supported strongly enough. Mode labels like co-op, multiplayer, and single-player are also compilation-wide and do not resolve the gameplay identity problem.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid because most candidates match the anchor as preservation-minded retro compilations of legacy licensed games rather than as single-loop action games. The best fits are other multi-game classic collections with version comparisons and museum/QoL framing. The weakest fit is the genre-specific Capcom bundle, which overlaps on arcade co-op brawlers but misses the anchor's broader licensed-compilation identity.
anchor_summary: MARVEL MaXimum Collection is best understood as a retro compilation/archive rather than a single-game archetype: a licensed package of multiple 8/16-bit and arcade-era Marvel games spanning different genres, with preservation framing, version completeness, co-op support in some included titles, and modern quality-of-life features like rewind, save states, galleries, and online support for select games.
gap_notes: The strongest commonality here is 'retro compilation/archive with modern extras,' not one unified combat loop or genre.
Collections that preserve multiple legacy versions of licensed games fit better than single-genre action anthologies.
Candidates centered on documentary/preservation framing are especially relevant because the anchor emphasizes archive/history features as much as play.

title: Dying Light: The Beast Restored Land

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify this as a long-form open-world Dying Light campaign focused on exploration of a large handcrafted region, first-person melee combat against infected, grounded parkour traversal, scavenging/loot, day-night survival pressure, authored story progression, and optional shared-progression co-op. Restored Land is described as an enhanced version of the same core game with harsher survival rules, permanent world consequences, and one-life options, which intensify survival elements without changing the underlying gameplay identity. That supports open_world_action_adventure as the primary archetype, with strong action_horror and exploration_survival_adventure traits.

overall_note: The rail has one essential inclusion and a couple of solid franchise/genre neighbors, but it misses stronger direct Dying Light comps and includes one candidate whose gameplay structure is notably farther away.
anchor_summary: Enhanced package of Dying Light: The Beast: a first-person open-world zombie action-survival game built around parkour traversal, physical melee combat, scavenging/crafting, day-night pressure, story progression, and optional co-op, with Restored Land adding harsher survival rules and permanence rather than changing the core gameplay identity.
gap_notes: Dying Light: The Beast is the clear must-keep because Restored Land is an enhanced edition of that base game.
Dying Light is a very strong franchise benchmark thanks to the same first-person parkour-melee-day/night identity.
Dying Light 2 Stay Human: Bloody Ties fits through shared core mechanics, but as a DLC-focused slice with a more specialized tournament structure it is less ideal than the full base game would be.
Dead Island 2 is a reasonable adjacent zombie melee comp, but it lacks the anchor's parkour-led traversal identity.
State of Decay 2 is meaningfully more management/survival-sim oriented and third-person, making it a weak rail fit.

candidate_reviews.rationale: This is the base game for the anchor's enhanced edition, with effectively the same open-world first-person parkour, melee survival, progression, and co-op structure.

title: Taito Milestones 4

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence describes Taito Milestones 4 as a retro arcade compilation containing ten different games across multiple distinct genres and play styles: sports minigames, martial arts/fighting, block-breaking/paddle action, platform action, shoot 'em up, maze/ball-rotation play, and beat-'em-up style action. The included games do not converge on a single stable gameplay identity that maps cleanly to one Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The Metacritic description is also contradictory/noisy, referring to an unrelated puzzle game ('ZOK'), which further weakens title-level archetype assignment. Because this is a mixed-genre compilation rather than one coherent game loop, classification into a single archetype would be misleading.

overall_note: The rail is directionally correct because it focuses on arcade compilations rather than forcing a single gameplay archetype. The same-series Taito Milestones entries are the clearest fits; broader publisher anthology comps are still valid but a bit less exact.
anchor_summary: Taito Milestones 4 is a mixed-genre retro arcade compilation rather than a single coherent game type, so the best similarities are other preservation-first arcade anthologies—especially other entries in the same Taito Milestones line with the same multi-game, short-session, coin-op structure.
gap_notes: Because the anchor is a compilation with many distinct genres, similarity should be judged at the package level: curated retro arcade anthology, short-run replayability, local pick-up-and-play variety, and preservation features.
The three Taito Milestones entries are the strongest candidates by far because they match the exact product format and audience expectation, not just retro aesthetics.
Capcom Arcade Stadium is a very good adjacent anthology comp, but its larger feature set and storefront-style packaging make it slightly less like a fixed ten-game Taito Milestones release.

Arcade Classics Anniversary Collection is a reasonable anthology comparison, but its lineup leans more heavily toward shooters/action and is a somewhat narrower gameplay mix than the anchor's broader spread.

title: Chico's Rebound

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game around skill-based block-breaking 'Rebound challenges' where the player keeps a walnut in play, manipulates shot angles with timed tail-whips, clears every block from stages, chases long volleys/high scores, and uses power-up interactions across varied obstacle layouts. That strongly supports a pinball-like arcade core rather than RPG or sports identity. The same copy also describes an interconnected six-floor tower between challenge stages, with environmental puzzles, unlocked paths, NPC help requests, secrets, bosses, and a linear mystery campaign, which supports metroidvania as a secondary influence rather than the primary loop. proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Copa City as a football-event tycoon/management game rather than a play-on-the-pitch sports title: 'economic strategy,' 'first football tycoon,' 'build in the city,' 'manage its structures and stadium,' and provide 'entertainment, food, and security' for fans, with success measured by 'Match Readiness.' The player organizes high-stakes football events, prepares urban space and stadium infrastructure, and manages club-specific fan behaviors and safety/fun tradeoffs. This strongly supports a management/tycoon identity with city-building and simulation strategy elements, using soccer as the theme rather than the primary play loop. Current taxonomy hints toward sports_sim are explained by football/match terminology, but the concrete gameplay loop is build/manage/optimize operations around matches, not directly competing in them.

title: Project Songbird

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store-page and Metacritic text consistently describe a first-person, short-form narrative horror game built around exploring forest and dreamscape environments while using ranged and melee weapons to survive. The copy explicitly emphasizes psychological horror, fighting to survive, upgradeable weapons, and a permadeath system that adds tension to encounters, which together support survival_horror more than purely psychological or walking-sim horror. The experience also appears authored, single-player, and linear rather than open-ended or match-based.

overall_note: The rail has several legitimate first-person horror comps, with the best fits matching Songbird’s psychological/narrative horror and combat-enabled survival loop. The main weakness is that the top candidate is actually DLC for Resident Evil 7 and leans more melee-focused/action-variant than the requested base-game comparison, making it a weaker fit than the strongest standalone candidates here.
anchor_summary: Project Songbird appears to be a short, first-person, single-player narrative survival horror game with psychological-horror framing, authored progression, exploration through natural and dreamlike spaces, ranged/melee combat, weapon upgrades, and meaningful survival pressure via permadeath.
gap_notes: Best matches are first-person single-player horror games that combine authored narrative progression with active combat or survival pressure.
The proposed #1 candidate is a DLC episode rather than Resident Evil 7 proper, so it is not an ideal benchmark for the anchor’s overall gameplay identity.
Some candidates skew more puzzle/adventure or sandbox/systemic than Songbird’s short cinematic authored structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on first-person psychological horror, authored story progression, surreal spaces, and regular combat presence; more puzzle/boss-structured than Songbird but still a strong gameplay neighbor.

Good overlap in first-person narrative horror, rural isolation, exploration, and intermittent combat, but it blends thriller/adventure elements and dual-timeline storytelling more heavily than Songbird’s tighter survival-horror loop.

title: EverSiege: Untold Ages

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently establish a hybrid 'hero-driven strategy rogue-lite' with procedural runs, solo or 3-player co-op, build synergies, troop summoning, city rebuilding, quests, bosses, and meta-style power growth. However, the evidence does not clearly resolve the game’s core playable form within the allowed archetypes: it could read as action-roguelite, RTS/strategy, or tactical hero-command play, and key identity axes such as perspective and primary control/interface are not concretely stated. Because the taxonomy lacks a clean action-strategy roguelite archetype and the available text does not strongly disambiguate toward any single allowed archetype, classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's mix of horde defense, rebuilding, and hero-led strategy fairly well, with The Riftbreaker and Thronefall standing out most. The main weakness is that several candidates match only one side of the blend: The Last Spell is much more turn-based/tactical, while Orcs Must Die! 3 is more trap-defense action than run-based kingdom strategy.
anchor_summary: EverSiege: Untold Ages appears to be a hybrid hero-driven strategy roguelite: direct hero buildcraft plus troop deployment, base/city rebuilding, horde defense, procedural run structure, boss pushes, and solo or small-group co-op. Best comps should preserve that action-strategy-defense blend rather than matching only fantasy setting or generic strategy labels.
gap_notes: A stronger rail would emphasize real-time or direct-control hero strategy hybrids with base rebuilding and wave defense.
Only some candidates reflect the anchor's roguelite/procedural run structure; others are closer to static campaign or stage-based defense games.
Co-op support matters for the anchor, and several otherwise solid candidates are single-player only.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Étrange Overlord as a frenetic real-time action RPG: players 'shoot, dodge, and slash' through battlefields, use a unique revolving-lane combat mechanic, and command from a roster of 15+ playable allies in 2-4 player co-op. That supports a combat-dominant, fast, stage-oriented action identity more than RPG subgenres centered on loot, open worlds, or party command from an overhead perspective. The text includes musical framing and songs, but it does not clearly establish rhythm-timing as the core gameplay loop; the current computed rhythm/party signals appear driven by thematic wording rather than concrete mechanics. Overall the strongest fit is character_action, with some beat-em-up overlap due to multiplayer crowd-fighting emphasis.

title: Screamer

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Screamer as an 'arcade racing game' centered on tournament-based car races in a futuristic anime-styled setting. Core loop evidence is strong and repeated across Steam and Metacritic: race on tracks, use vehicles with unique abilities, juggle Boost/Strike/Shield, compete in multiple race modes including Team Races, and play online or split-screen. The dominant identity is vehicular racing with arcade combat embellishments rather than a fighting game proper, supporting arcade_racer over party_game or realistic_racer.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: all four candidates are legitimate arcade-racing neighbors, and the top three especially fit Screamer's race-plus-combat identity. The main gap is that several candidates lean pickup/weapon combat or Burnout-style takedowns rather than Screamer's character/ability-driven fighting systems and mode mix, so the set is solid but not perfect.
anchor_summary: Screamer is a combat-leaning arcade racer: closed-track car racing with aggressive offensive/defensive abilities, boost management, knockout pressure, multiple competitive race modes including team-focused variants, and both online and split-screen play. The best comps are arcade racers where winning races is tightly fused with vehicular combat rather than pure sim handling or open-world driving.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-identical benchmark for Screamer's specific blend of arcade circuit racing and bespoke driver abilities.

title: Life is Strange: Reunion

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify a single-player, third-person, story-driven Life is Strange game centered on alternating between Max and Chloe, making consequential dialogue decisions, rewinding time, investigating suspects, and solving environmental/four-dimensional puzzles. However, that evidence points most strongly to a narrative adventure / choice-driven adventure identity that is not represented cleanly in the allowed archetype list. The game does not show sufficient evidence for visual_novel, cinematic_action_adventure, immersive_sim_action, or other available archetypes without forcing a poor fit. Because the core gameplay identity is clear but falls into a taxonomy gap rather than an existing supported archetype, this title should be rejected instead of misclassified.

overall_note: The candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity: story-led third-person adventure, branching dialogue, supernatural investigation, light puzzle solving, and minimal combat emphasis. The rail is strongest when it foregrounds other Life is Strange entries and Tell Me Why as a close external neighbor.
anchor_summary: Life is Strange: Reunion is a single-player, third-person, choice-driven narrative adventure centered on conversation decisions, character relationships, light exploration, environmental/time-manipulation puzzles, and supernatural mystery. Its closest comps should share the cinematic story-adventure loop more than just tone or franchise.
gap_notes: The strongest fit is the direct predecessor Life is Strange: Double Exposure, which should lead the rail.

Life is Strange: Before the Storm is also a strong fit despite lacking Max's rewind focus, because it shares the same conversational, exploration-heavy, consequence-driven adventure loop.
Tell Me Why is the best non-franchise neighbor thanks to near-identical narrative adventure structure and light puzzle/mystery emphasis.

title: Mega Man Star Force Legacy Collection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence confirms this is a compilation with single-player and online PvP support, battles, Bonus Cards, and some narrative setup around Geo Stelar and Omega-Xis. However, the provided DB text does not concretely establish the core gameplay loop, perspective, exploration structure, combat format, or progression model strongly enough to map it to a supported V2 archetype. 'Battle Cards' suggests some card-based system, but the evidence does not show whether cards are the primary play structure versus an action RPG, tactical RPG, or hybrid system. Because the bundle lacks sufficiently specific gameplay identity signals, classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The rail has one obvious must-keep comp, one smart gameplay-neighbor pick, and one franchise-adjacent but materially looser recommendation.
anchor_summary: Mega Man Star Force Legacy Collection is a compilation of seven portable-era action RPGs centered on real-time battles using collectible Battle Cards, with single-player campaigns and added online PvP plus collection/QoL extras. The closest fits should match that hybrid of exploration/progression and lane or grid-based real-time card combat, not just Mega Man branding or compilation format.
gap_notes: Mega Man Battle Network Legacy Collection is the clearest benchmark and should anchor this rail.
One Step From Eden is a strong mechanical neighbor, but its roguelike run structure makes it less identity-matched than Battle Network/Star Force.
Mega Man Zero/ZX Legacy Collection shares publisher, character legacy, and collection framing, but its core gameplay is action-platforming rather than card-based action RPG combat.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest comp: another Mega Man collection built around exploration, progression, and real-time grid/card combat with deck customization and online legacy features. Extremely overlapping audience and play identity.

Reasonable only as a franchise/collection neighbor. Its core loop is challenging action-platforming and boss mastery, not exploration with card-driven real-time tactical battles, so gameplay fit is loose.

title: SHINONOME ABYSS The Maiden Exorcist

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently establish a horror-themed single-player roguelike about escaping procedurally changing haunted mansions using limited items, traps, environmental hazards, clue-reading, and survival across three modes including fixed-map puzzle scenarios and endless survival. However, the evidence does not cleanly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It suggests a hybrid of horror action, survival, puzzle-solving, and roguelike structure, but key archetype-defining signals are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective/view, no clear combat model beyond trap use and occasional empowered state, and no strong fit to survival_horror, action_horror, psychological_horror, turn_based_tactics, or immersive_sim_action as defined by the available taxonomy. Because the gameplay identity appears materially hybrid and the closest archetypes are only partial matches, classification would be too speculative.

overall_note: The set has a solid core of roguelike dungeon-crawler comps, led by very strong Mystery Dungeon-style matches, but it gets looser once it shifts toward horror-only evasion or broad procedural challenge games. Stronger rails would prioritize knowledge-heavy roguelike survival and tactical item/trap play over atmosphere alone.
anchor_summary: Single-player horror roguelike about escaping procedurally changing haunted mansions through cautious room-to-room exploration, limited items and traps, environmental exploitation, clue-reading, puzzle scenarios, and high-stakes survival modes. Best comps should emphasize run-based dungeon survival, trap/item tactics, and knowledge-driven progression more than just horror theme.
gap_notes: The best-fitting identity here is run-based roguelike dungeon survival with item/trap management; only a few candidates strongly cover that full loop.
Horror-only neighbors like Yomawari capture threat evasion and spirit danger, but miss the anchor's procedural roguelike structure and survival-mode progression.
Spelunky 2 shares procedural danger, traps, and knowledge-based survival, but its real-time platforming and multiplayer profile make it a notably looser fit than the dungeon-crawler candidates.
No direct base-game or near-identical companion is present; the rail relies on adjacent genre neighbors rather than obvious same-identity matches.

title: DAMON and BABY

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as an action-adventure centered on exploration plus highly technical twin-stick shooting, alternating between an exploration phase and a home-base upgrade/shop phase, with weapon pickups, skill customization, boss battles, and optional local co-op. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly fit the allowed archetype set: it is not a beat 'em up, and the evidence is too mixed for open_world_action_adventure because the copy also frames progression around clearing stages/areas and boss encounters rather than a clearly quest-web open world structure. The result is a strong description of the game, but not a strong enough match to an existing taxonomy archetype.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally right because it focuses on top-down action games with shooting, exploration, progression, and co-op. The best fits are games that combine twin-stick combat, area progression, loot/upgrades, and boss encounters. The weaker entries drift toward broader action-RPG or melee-forward adventure structures rather than the anchor’s more shooter-centric exploration loop.
anchor_summary: Top-down/twin-stick action-adventure built around exploration across connected areas, ranged shooting with some close-range options, boss-centric stage clearing, weapon pickups, skill customization, a home-base upgrade/shop loop, and optional local co-op.
gap_notes: The anchor is specifically described as highly technical twin-stick shooting plus exploration; candidates that are melee-led or broad action-RPGs are looser fits.
Home-base upgrade/shop pacing and stage/boss-clear structure are distinctive anchor traits that only some candidates mirror well.
Local co-op matters, but should not outweigh combat identity and progression structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on twin-stick shooting, exploration through dense zones, loot, builds, and co-op. More RPG/quest-heavy and cover-based than the anchor, but still the strongest gameplay match.

Excellent top-down shooter comp with co-op, weapon variety, character growth, area clearing, and big combat focus. Less exploration/adventure-oriented than the anchor, but the combat loop is very close.
Shares overhead perspective, action-adventure progression, boss fights, exploration, and co-op. However, it is more melee-forward, puzzle/adventure-driven, and less twin-stick-shooter-centric.

Has overworld exploration, build customization, dungeons, bosses, and co-op, but its form-swapping action-RPG identity is much farther from the anchor’s technical twin-stick shooting and weapon-driven combat.

title: Overpowered 2 - Crux of Fate

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify a top-down retro pixel-art single-player survival shooter focused on surviving enemy waves in a confined arena, collecting XP drops, auto-attacking with acquired weapons, and gaining stronger odds through repeated failed attempts. This is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but that identity is a survivorlike / bullet-heaven style auto-attacking horde survival game, which is not represented by the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list. Available archetypes such as arena_fps, loot_shooter, action_horror, co_op_action_roguelite, or extraction_shooter would materially misclassify the core loop.

overall_note: The proposed rail is very strong and centered on true survivorlike comps. It correctly prioritizes genre-defining and near-identical horde-survival games, with only the series predecessor landing a bit lower because it appears to be more manual twin-stick mission/survival action than full auto-attacking bullet-heaven design.
anchor_summary: Top-down single-player survivorlike / bullet-heaven arena shooter: movement-focused kiting in a confined arena, weapons auto-attack, enemies drop XP for mid-run level-ups and added weapons, runs are wave-based, and repeated failures provide meta-progression via increased luck.
gap_notes: The set captures the anchor's gameplay identity well: auto-attacking horde survival, XP pickups, run-based upgrades, and meta progression.

title: Ariana and the Elder Codex

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored gameplay text is sufficient and consistent for a metroidvania classification. The Steam detailed description explicitly describes a 2D platformer structure with combat and ability-gated exploration: Ariana defeats enemies to earn new magic spells and abilities that grant access to previously unreachable areas, takes on platforming and combat challenges, and fights formidable bosses. This strongly supports side-scrolling traversal, platform navigation, boss-driven combat, and progression via new abilities opening the map over time, which is the core identity of metroidvania. The seven codices framing suggests segmented but exploratory spaces rather than purely linear stages, so semi_open is the best fit. Secondary action_platformer is also supported, but the explicit unreachable-area gating and ability unlock loop make metroidvania the stronger primary archetype.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong because every candidate is a 2D action-platformer/metroidvania with combat and ability-driven exploration. Best fits emphasize spell or loadout-based combat, boss challenge, and gated progression; weaker fits diverge through much harsher bullet-hell emphasis or combo-brawler structure.
anchor_summary: Ariana and the Elder Codex appears to be a single-player 2D action-platforming metroidvania: side-scrolling traversal, enemy and boss combat, ability unlocks that open previously unreachable areas, spell loadout customization, and craft/equipable items that support progression and challenge.
gap_notes: No candidate is an obvious base-game pairing or exact gameplay twin for Ariana.
The best matches are metroidvanias with ability-gated exploration and strong boss/platforming loops.
Several candidates match the structure well but differ in combat feel: some are more soulslike-punishing, others more combo-brawler or bullet-hell heavy.
Ariana's notable spell-slot customization and crafted/equipped item layer are only partially mirrored by the candidate set.

title: Jesus Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Jesus Simulator as a 'narrative visual novel experience' that retells the Gospel through dialogue, key story moments, multiple viewpoints, and interactive mini-games. The text emphasizes authored historical narrative, spiritual reflection, and witnessing events rather than combat, systems-driven simulation, or open-ended RPG play. Single-player mode is directly supported by store metadata. Although one metadata label mentions 'First-Person Adventure' and generic RPG/simulation tags appear, the detailed gameplay copy is much more specific and strongly supports visual_novel as the core identity.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's historical, educational, and narrative-first qualities reasonably well, but several candidates lean more toward adventure, puzzle, or RPG structures than true visual-novel-style progression.

title: Moto Rush Reborn

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify a level-based motorcycle racing game centered on clearing fast-paced tracks, weaving through traffic, avoiding obstacles, chasing record times, and completing skill objectives. The core loop is explicitly vehicular racing with vehicle control, speed management, track hazards, unlockable levels, and score/time-performance goals rather than simulation realism. The copy also describes 45 levels, collectible symbols, and chapter unlocks, supporting an arcade-style campaign structure with replayable stage mastery. '3D runner' phrasing adds obstacle-dodging flavor, but the dominant identity remains arcade racing.

overall_note: The set contains two clearly relevant skill-racing comps and two looser neighbors. Best matches emphasize stage-based motorcycle control, obstacle navigation, and repeat-run mastery; weaker fits drift toward combat-driving or simpler lane-dodging arcade play.
anchor_summary: Moto Rush Reborn is a single-player, level-based arcade motorcycle game built around high-speed obstacle avoidance, lane-splitting through traffic, jumps/slides/ducking, time-performance goals, and replaying stages for mastery and collectibles. Its identity sits between arcade racing and 3D runner-style reflex challenge rather than simulation or combat racing.
gap_notes: The rail is missing a closer benchmark in the motorcycle endless-runner/obstacle-course lane rather than broader car racers.

title: RUBATO

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official text is sufficient and consistent. Steam explicitly calls RUBATO an 'experimental, 2D physics-based take on the collect-a-thon genre' and says players move through 'open metroidvania-like areas' across '5 compact, densely packed areas' while uncovering 'secrets and shortcuts in every corner of the world.' That strongly supports a side-scrolling exploration-platformer structure with semi-open interconnected spaces and discovery gating, which fits metroidvania better than the 3d_collectathon archetype because the game is explicitly 2D. The description also mentions interacting with enemies and objects ('use your tongue to lick enemies and objects') and collecting Planet Bits to fix the solar system, supporting moderate action layered onto exploration. Narrative framing is authored and character-focused, but gameplay identity is primarily side-scrolling exploratory platforming.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's exploratory platforming and offbeat collectathon/metroidvania blend reasonably well. Yoku's Island Express is the standout fit because it shares the unusual physics-forward 2D exploration identity. The rest are viable but looser due to either stronger combat/action-metroidvania emphasis, broader adventure structure, or a perspective mismatch.
anchor_summary: RUBATO appears to be a single-player 2D exploratory platformer with collectathon goals, physics-driven interactions, compact semi-open areas, secret hunting, and some metroidvania-style ability/path progression. The closest matches should emphasize side-scrolling exploration, traversal/collection as the core loop, and quirky action-adventure structure over pure combat or purely 3D collectathon play.
gap_notes: No candidate is a truly near-identical benchmark for a 2D physics-based collectathon-metroidvania hybrid, so the rail lacks a clear 'same' comp.
The set leans partly toward general whimsical exploration games; some candidates miss the anchor's collectible-driven structure or 2D-specific traversal identity.
Cavern of Dreams captures dense collectathon exploration well, but its 3D camera and N64-style platformer structure make it notably less precise than the 2D candidates.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit here: 2D exploration-platforming with a physics-centric movement model, collectible/ability progression, and interconnected non-linear world structure.

Very solid on 2D interconnected exploration and ability-gated progression, but Ori is more action-platformer/metroidvania and less physics-driven, comedic, or collectathon-centered than RUBATO.
Strong overlap in dense secret hunting, collectible progression, compact worlds, and ability unlocks, but the 3D perspective and classic 3D collectathon structure make it a looser gameplay match.

title: Inky Blinky Bob

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently describe a single-player action-horror campaign built around piloting a weaponized hot air balloon across a chain of islands, scavenging resources, repairing and upgrading the vehicle, managing limited med kits, and fighting failed experiments with mounted weapons plus close-range axe/shotgun combat. The game emphasizes survival pressure, hostile horror theming, freely flying between islands, and objective/gig-based progression toward powering towers and reaching a sealed facility. This strongly supports action_horror as the best fit, with survival-adventure and open-world action-adventure traits as secondary influences. Perspective and exact camera are not stated, so those fields are left empty.

overall_note: The rail has a strong center around survival games with a mobile upgradable vehicle/base, which matches the anchor's most distinctive loop. The best fits emphasize vehicle-first traversal plus scavenging, repairs, upgrades, and combat. Weaker entries drift toward lighter combatless vehicle adventure or more generic survival crafting without the anchor's action-horror pressure.

title: Ghost Master: Resurrection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player horror-themed strategy/adventure game centered on commanding specters, solving puzzles, and resource management across distinct locations. However, the available text does not cleanly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The gameplay reads closest to a god-game / tactical puzzle / strategy hybrid, which is outside the permitted archetype list. While there are strong signals for horror setting, tactical optimization, and unit control, key identity facets needed to force-fit an allowed archetype are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective, no clear RTS base-building/economy loop, no turn-based tactics framing, no shooter/action framing, and combat itself appears indirect rather than a dominant conventional combat loop. Given the apparent taxonomy gap and lack of a strong allowable match, rejection is the safest outcome.

overall_note: The rail has one clearly strong management-side comp and one looser but still understandable adjacent management comp, but neither fully captures Ghost Master's scenario-based indirect haunting/puzzle identity. title: RUSHING BEAT X: Return Of Brawl Brothers

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and directly identify the game as a beat 'em up/brawler: 'ultimate Beat 'em up,' '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' with 9 stages, enemy-filled areas, 2-player co-op story mode, multiple playable fighters, auto-combos, counterattacks, follow-up strikes, throws, jumps, dashes, cancel system, rage gauge, and a finishing move. This strongly supports a classic side-scrolling stage-based beat_em_up identity with dominant melee combat and combo-driven crowd encounters.

overall_note: This is a very strong candidate set for a modern arcade-style beat 'em up. The top three are highly credible rails for the anchor's core gameplay identity, while River City Girls 2 is still relevant but looser because its broader progression and exploratory structure pull it away from a straight stage-based brawler comparison.
anchor_summary: RUSHING BEAT X: Return Of Brawl Brothers is a classic side-scrolling beat 'em up: stage-based urban progression, melee crowd control, throws, weapons, dash/jump cancels, character-specific movesets, rage meter, and local 2-player co-op. Best matches should be other belt-scrolling brawlers with similarly arcade-structured combat and combo-focused hand-to-hand play.
gap_notes: No obvious wrong-title inclusion; all four candidates are legitimate beat 'em up neighbors.

Very solid classic arcade brawler fit with urban stages, weapon pickups, throws, distinct characters, and local co-op; a bit more stripped-down and old-school in structure than the anchor's broader systems.

title: The Coin Game

The evidence is strong about the game's premise, but it does not cleanly align with any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. Top computed candidates like sports_sim, pinball, and party_game are partial fits at best and fail to capture the product's core identity as an open-world arcade/minigame sandbox. overall_note: The rail has one clear must-surface comparison and two looser but still understandable neighbors. title: Basketball Classics

title: Dynasty Warriors 3 Complete Edition Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Dynasty Warriors-style '1 vs. 1,000 action' on battlefields with over 40 officers, True Musou Attacks, evolved action combat, officer development, challenge modes, and two-player simultaneous play. Metacritic explicitly labels it a '3D Beat-'Em-Up,' which aligns with the core loop of third-person melee crowd-clearing combat across staged battles. The game also includes officer progression and tactical battlefield framing, supporting a light secondary resemblance to character action, but the dominant identity is musou/beat-'em-up style melee crowd control.

overall_note: The set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity. All three candidates are legitimate musou comparisons, with another mainline Dynasty Warriors package as the clearest top comp, followed by close franchise neighbors from Samurai Warriors and Warriors Orochi.
anchor_summary: Dynasty Warriors 3 Complete Edition Remastered is a classic Omega Force musou: third-person melee-heavy 1-vs-1,000 battlefield missions with officer-based movesets, story campaigns, light character progression, tactical map pressure, and local co-op/PvP side modes. Best matches should preserve that musou crowd-clearing combat loop and staged battle structure, not just historical setting.
gap_notes: A direct Dynasty Warriors 3 original/Xtreme Legends comp or another early mainline Dynasty Warriors remaster would be even more exact if available.

title: Crimson Desert

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crimson Desert as a third-person open-world action-adventure/RPG set in a medieval fantasy world, with free exploration across wilderness, cities, ruins, and the Abyss. Core play includes questing, battles, challenging enemies and bosses, multiple weapons and skills, elemental enhancements, dodges/counters/grapples, and traversal via climbing, gliding, and horseback. Progression includes character growth, skill development, equipment, and rebuilding/upgrading the Greymane camp, supporting an action-RPG identity over pure action-adventure. The strongest fit is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, with secondary overlap into open_world_action_adventure and some soulslike signals from punishing boss-focused combat.

overall_note: The set has several credible open-world fantasy action RPG neighbors. Dragon's Dogma 2 and Elden Ring are the clearest fits on gameplay identity, while The Witcher 3 and Dragon's Dogma: Dark Arisen remain solid but somewhat looser. Black Desert Online shares combat feel and side activities, but its MMORPG structure makes it a much weaker rail fit.
anchor_summary: Crimson Desert looks like a third-person single-player open-world fantasy action RPG: freeform exploration, quest-driven structure, real-time melee/hybrid combat, challenging bosses, character/build growth, mounts and traversal, plus some camp/settlement progression.
gap_notes: No exact same-game/base-game style pairing is present, so the rail tops out at strong neighbors rather than an obvious must-keep benchmark.

title: Scritchy Scratchy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Scritchy Scratchy as a scratch-card incremental/idle-style progression game centered on buying scratch-offs, revealing symbols, unlocking auto-scratching, taking risks on higher-variance cards, and prestiging for permanent upgrades. That establishes a clear gameplay identity, but it does not map cleanly to any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not supported as pinball, racer, sports, card battler, management tycoon, or other listed archetypes, and forcing a fit would misclassify the title. Rejecting is appropriate because the game appears to sit outside the current archetype set rather than lacking evidence.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around idle/incremental progression games, with Luck be a Landlord and Cookie Clicker as the best fits. It gets weaker when candidates are included mainly for gambling flavor or broad numbers-go-up appeal rather than the anchor’s specific idle scratcher loop.
anchor_summary: Scritchy Scratchy is a single-player scratch-card incremental/idle game built around repeated scratching, luck manipulation, automation unlocks, high-variance risk/reward choices, and prestige resets for permanent progression.
gap_notes: Best matches should emphasize incremental/idle progression, automation, and prestige rather than just casino aesthetics.
The anchor is not a deckbuilder or score-attack roguelike, so poker/card-synergy comps overstate similarity.
Combat-centric clickers are only partial fits because Scritchy Scratchy lacks enemy clearing and action pressure.
A stronger rail would lean further into idle/incremental economy builders with passive automation and reset-based progression.

title: DigDigDrill

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present DigDigDrill as a single-player underground mining and resource-collection game focused on digging for minerals and treasure, returning to the surface when inventory is full, and forging/customizing drills through a puzzle-like smithing/build-synergy system. The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. The dominant loop appears to be relaxed resource gathering, build crafting, and progression through deeper floors, but the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clear non-combat mining / digging / crafting progression archetype that this cleanly fits. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's mining-and-upgrade loop, but most candidates add strong combat, survival pressure, or metroidvania/sandbox structure that changes the feel substantially. There are usable neighbors here, but no truly same-identity match.
anchor_summary: DigDigDrill is a single-player underground mining and loot-collection game built around repeated descent, filling your bag, returning to the surface, and forging customized drills with synergistic effects. Its identity is notably non-combat and low-pressure: no enemies during core mining and no time limits, with progression driven by grinding, item collection, and build crafting rather than survival or action challenge.
gap_notes: The anchor's strongest distinguishing trait is relaxed mining without enemies or timers; most candidates center combat or defense pressure.
Inventory-run extraction plus surface return and gear-forging progression is represented, but usually inside different macro structures like metroidvania, roguelite defense, or sandbox survival.
A closer rail would favor low-pressure digging/crafting grinders over combat-heavy mining hybrids.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best match for dig-deeper resource runs and upgrade-driven underground progression. It differs by being a combat-heavy platform metroidvania, but mining and equipment-led advancement still overlap strongly.
Shares underground mining, ore collection, crafting, and long-term power growth. Fit is loosened by its sandbox-survival/base-building focus, multiplayer profile, and much broader activity set.
The dig-haul-return-upgrade loop is relevant, but the game's core identity is wave-defense roguelike survival with constant time pressure, which clashes with DigDigDrill's enemy-free relaxed mining.
Mining for resources and upgrading gear are shared, but the roguelite run structure and recurring monster-defense demands make it a more action-defense game than a relaxed mining/crafting grinder.

title: Defending Camelot

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Defending Camelot as a tower defense game explicitly compared to Plants vs. Zombies, with 160 campaign levels, unit unlocking, coin collection, power-ups, and waves of monsters prevented from entering Camelot. That is strong evidence for tower defense gameplay, but tower defense is not represented by a suitable archetype in the provided Similar Games Taxonomy V2 list. Available archetypes like rts or side_scrolling_action_strategy would misclassify the core gameplay identity.

overall_note: The set has two excellent tower-defense comps and one solid broader TD neighbor, but it also includes a major mismatch from the Plants vs. Zombies franchise that is actually a shooter rather than a lane-defense strategy game.
anchor_summary: Defending Camelot is a campaign-focused tower defense game explicitly described as similar to Plants vs. Zombies, centered on lane/style defensive unit placement, wave stopping, unit unlocks, coin-based progression, power-ups, and many levels with stage modifiers.
gap_notes: Plants vs. Zombies is the clearest benchmark and should rank at the top.

Kingdom Rush is a very strong tower defense neighbor with campaign progression, enemy counters, and ability use, though its path-based structure is a bit less directly aligned than PvZ-style lane defense.
PixelJunk Monsters 2 is a valid but looser tower defense comp due to its more freeform real-time map movement and co-op emphasis.
Plants vs. Zombies: Garden Warfare 2 is not a good fit; shared franchise/theme does not outweigh its third-person shooter identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The defining comp: lane-based tower defense, staged waves, varied defender roster, progression through many levels, and explicit audience overlap.
A very close tower defense match with campaign structure, unit/tower specialization, spell support, enemy-type counterplay, and long-form level progression.
Clearly in the same tower defense family, but its freer map interaction, top-down control, and co-op-focused presentation make it a somewhat looser fit than PvZ or Kingdom Rush.
Despite the franchise name, this is a character-based shooter with co-op/PvP combat, quests, and ability loadouts rather than lane-defense tower defense gameplay.

title: The Artisan of Glimmith

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports a non-combat, cozy single-player handcrafted puzzle game focused on coloring stained glass, rule-based logic puzzles, secret optional puzzles, and a level editor. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not include a general logic/puzzle-game bucket. The system-generated near miss for hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the actual description, which emphasizes rule-based handcrafted puzzles rather than object-finding scenes. Because the strongest evidence indicates a puzzle identity that does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of the anchor's non-combat, handcrafted puzzle identity and does best when it emphasizes elegant rule discovery, optional secrets, and relaxed exploration. Its strongest candidates are broader deduction/exploration puzzle games rather than aesthetic matches. The weakest inclusion leans heavily on stained-glass theme while missing the anchor's core rule-learning progression.
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player handcrafted logic-puzzle game centered on coloring and assembling stained-glass artworks, with rule-based puzzle progression, optional hidden/finale challenges, no combat, and light exploratory structure.
gap_notes: The set captures handcrafted logic-puzzle design well, but only one candidate reflects the anchor's stained-glass/art-construction aspect, and that one is mechanically looser.
The best fits are exploration-driven or deduction-heavy puzzle games; none clearly mirror the anchor's specific color/cut/join artwork restoration loop.
No candidate addresses the level-editor/community-sharing angle, so the rail reflects campaign play more than creation features.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent fit on handcrafted solo puzzle progression, layered rule discovery, exploration, and optional secrets. Less cozy/artisanal and more open-world first-person, but the mastery curve is very aligned.
Strong match for relaxed, curated, non-combat puzzle play with elegant mechanics, discovery, and off-path optional content. Its push-block/island structure is different, but audience overlap is high.

Very good pure-logic comparison: hand-designed, rule-complete deduction with escalating challenge and no guessing. However, it lacks the anchor's cozy presentation, exploratory structure, and artistic restoration feel.

Strong thematic and visual overlap through stained-glass assembly and relaxed pacing, but mechanically it is much closer to artistic jigsaw puzzling than rule-based logic progression. Understandable comp, not a close gameplay match.

title: Retro Rewind - Video Store Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store descriptions consistently present a business-management simulator loop: rent and sell movies, organize inventory, hire staff, manage returns and fees, decorate and expand the shop, and respond to seasonal customer demand. This strongly supports management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue rather than urban planning or autonomous colony survival. Evidence also clearly supports single-player, non-combat, sandbox-style progression with base expansion and sim-oriented challenges.

overall_note: The rail is directionally correct: both candidates are hands-on first-person business management sims with direct customer service, store renovation, staffing, and expansion. Supermarket Simulator is a very close fit; Gas Station Simulator is solid but a bit looser because its loop broadens into forecourt/service-station operations rather than tightly focused retail inventory-and-rental management.
anchor_summary: Retro Rewind - Video Store Simulator is a single-player, non-combat management sim centered on hands-on retail operations: stocking and organizing inventory, serving customers, processing returns/fees, hiring staff, decorating, and expanding a specialty store through a sandbox business-growth loop.
gap_notes: The strongest fit here is another first-person retail/store-management sim with manual stocking, cashier/service tasks, and base expansion.

title: Thomas & Friends: Wonders of Sodor

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator copy consistently describes a train-driving simulation built around taking the controls of Thomas and other engines from an interactive first-person cab view. The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

overall_note: The proposed rail is very tight. Both candidates match the anchor's underlying gameplay foundation almost exactly: first-person cab operation, route/service structure, simulation-focused train handling, and similar mode profiles. The main difference is tone and accessibility, not core loop.
anchor_summary: A single-player first-person train-driving simulation built around operating rail services from the cab, with story scenarios, timetable runs, shunting, free roam, and accessible-but-simulation-oriented controls. gap_notes: The strongest similarity signal is that the anchor is explicitly powered by Train Sim World, making mainline Train Sim World entries highly expected recommendations.

title: Maid of Sker VR

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

overall_note: The rail has one essential inclusion and a couple of reasonable VR horror neighbors, but it drifts toward broader immersive horror rather than tightly matching Maid of Sker VR's stealth-survival loop. One candidate is notably misleading because it is a combat-heavy DLC rather than the requested base game.
anchor_summary: VR-only first-person survival horror centered on stealthy evasion, noise/breath management, environmental interaction, puzzle-solving, clue-driven exploration, and limited nonlethal defense inside a hostile enclosed setting.
gap_notes: The non-VR Maid of Sker is the clear must-keep benchmark and should anchor the rail.

title: Royal Revolt Survivors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

payload.anchor_summary: Royal Revolt Survivors is a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite centered on horde clearing, per-run build stacking, hero differentiation, boss encounters, and a notable 1-4 player co-op mode.

gap_notes: The strongest matches on Survivor-style horde loop are mostly single-player, so the rail underrepresents the anchor's co-op identity.
The strongest matches on 1-4 player top-down co-op action roguelite lean more room/objective-driven than pure Survivor-style arena survival.
Overall selection is relevant, but there is no near-perfect 'same' candidate that combines co-op, top-down action roguelite structure, and explicit Survivor-like swarm progression.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on horde-survival action roguelite loop, boss focus, build synergies, and multiple characters, but weaker mode fit since it is primarily single-player rather than 1-4 co-op.
A core benchmark for Survivor-style runs, upgrade stacking, and overwhelming swarms; less aligned on active melee/ranged hero combat and boss-centric co-op emphasis.
Top-down 1-4 co-op roguelike action with distinct heroes, horde fights, and boss runs matches the anchor's party-based action well, though its map/objective structure is less purely Survivor-like.
Strong co-op/top-down/action-roguelite overlap with build synergies and bosses, but it plays more like a room-based hack-and-slash roguelite than a Survivor-style swarm survival game.
Good match on horde survival, hero builds, and boss encounters, but the single-player focus and more Diablo-like retro action framing make it a looser fit than the others.

title: Grind Survivors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is highly aligned with the anchor’s gameplay identity. The best fits are other survivors-like horde-action roguelites with run-based progression, heavy buildcraft, and arena survival loops; the only slight looseness comes from candidates that lean more manual twin-stick shooting or inter-wave shop structure.
anchor_summary: Grind Survivors is a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite built around repeated runs, dense horde pressure, bullet-hell dodging, rapid upgrade stacking, procedural weapon loot, and endless scaling survival.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are all survivors-like horde survival roguelites, which is the anchor’s clearest gameplay lane despite taxonomy gaps.
Candidates with stronger manual aiming or wave/shop segmentation are still relevant, but a bit less exact than freeform survivors-like snowball runs.
No obvious bad candidate is present; the set mainly differs in degree of structural closeness.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close match on survivors-like arena loop, horde clearing, bullet-heavy dodging, deep build synergies, weapon crafting, unlock-based progression, and endless power scaling.
Genre-defining survivors-like comp with the same run-based survival structure, escalating swarms, upgrade drafting, and snowball progression, even if its presentation is more minimalist.
Very close on dark horde survival, repeated 30-minute runs, hero/build synergies, meta progression, and boss-inflected wave survival; slightly more Diablo-like in feel.
Strong fit through top-down arena survival, fast runs, heavy stat/buildcraft, and overwhelming enemy waves, though its discrete wave/shop cadence is a bit less like continuous survivors-style flow.

Good overlap in short-run survival roguelite structure, dense enemy pressure, dodging, and weapon-centered builds, but it leans more manual twin-stick shooting than typical auto-attack survivors-like play.
